speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Mary Gray Edwards took place at her apartment in Moscow on November 3rd 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well in those days when you were a kid there, did you get into town much?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: You didn't go anyplace. If you went anyplace you went all horseback. You walked. And if the family were good, that is, if well say my mother and dad, some of the kids were going. You had a surrey. You filled your surrey. You had a friend on top. Good. Sure did.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And about the only time I can remember I was in a marnette, but I remember going to my grandmother's funeral. Because we had this surrey fool. And we came to Genesee in the cemetery. And I knew the neighbors because you thought nothing of walking. Of course we were old, about two and a half or three miles from school. And I walked a great deal of time, sometimes a little horseback in the bed. Just depends, you know. But we thought nothing of walking to school. I was pretty small and we had a pretty steep hill. And sometimes my brother would carry me part way up that hill. My older brother. But of course when we moved to town, when I was still pretty young, I was at old, I suppose, twelve, thirteen, I think, somewhere in there. I really don't know.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I know someone was saying the other day, they were talking about Bathtub. And so they couldn't remember ever not having a bathtub. I say, I remember when they didn't have a bathtub. You pumped the water. There's no other way of getting it. You carried it up, heated it in the boiler, and put it in the tub and had your bath. I said, they did. There wasn't such a thing as a bathtub.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would each kid do that whole thing over again or did a bunch of kids use the same water?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, I don't think I think that. I suppose when I was small enough, my brother probably pumped it for me. But I was still pretty young when I used to do that myself. Because it didn't take too much because your tubs were only about so big. And if you have to, you can take a bath in not too much water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that tub shape like?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Perfectly round. And it was about like that. They used the washing machine. You, well, you wore some on the board. But far back, I can remember, they had a washing machine that you went this way. You did it by hand. And then it went through and then you used this tub to rinse them. And they had two of them. You'd rinse it into one, and you'd rinse it into the next water to get the soap out.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And, well, and they, you know, one thing I think of, you know, as a kid, I never heard an unkind word about a neighbor or anyone criticize anybody. And that never dawned on to me until recently. And now people are always talking so much, you know, and saying something about somebody. Maybe it's true, maybe it isn't. But when I was a kid, I thought everybody was, I guess, perfect. Because there wasn't anyone, and I knew them for a few miles around because we went to school, they called it the Graygle District. And there were programs and had church services now and then in another area that was east. And, well, we usually went a horseback or maybe we'd go in a buggy or some way, and we'd go down there to that. And it was always a good turn off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To go there, you would go to church services there, too, you say?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They had church services sometimes, not very often, but I guess they were kind of special.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But never an unkind word.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean that you think your parents really never, even among themselves, between themselves, would talk about other people?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't think so. I don't think they did because I never heard anything until after we moved into town. And I got to know people in town. Of course, I was getting older. Then once in a while, you'd hear a little something, but not very much. It seemed like the older I got. It's when you begin to hear people talk about somebody. There was something wrong with this one or that one, when maybe they weren't as bad as the one that was doing the talking. But as a child, I absolutely didn't think there was anything wrong with anybody, because the people that I knew never said anything unkind that I ever heard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or ever heard anyone calling anybody out?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And as I remember, I don't remember the teacher having any trouble with the kids. I can remember one thing, the only punishment I ever remember of is when maybe they were supposed to be quiet, to take an exam or something, and somebody whispered, go up and stand in the front of the room with your back to the class. I don't know any punishment I ever saw in school when I was a kid. I never saw anything. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the kids always behaved their parents?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They must.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That they always behaved their parents then?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I would say they were dead. I can't remember if my folks ever say no about anything, because, well, we weren't tempted to do anything that wasn't right. There wasn't any reason that I looked back at it. And I just wonder sometimes about it, because I never heard of anything about any of the neighbors out there ever doing anything that wasn't right, or saying anything mean about anybody.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I always thought everybody was fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think everybody was?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I do. I really do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think they really were almost perfect?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes, I do, because there was no occasion. And there was nothing to…

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I don't know. I can remember one time my sister corrected me for something. She was going to give me a spanking. She went out and got a switch. She came in. Of course, she was a lot older than I. And I looked up at her, and of course, I was really pretty active for it. So she answered, and I grabbed that thing, and I broke it and took it through it out in the yard. She walked off. She never said another word. It was dropped right there.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I can look back and think of some of the things they said and done. Now I appreciate it, because I was so small, and I didn't like it because my brothers… Well, most of them, of course, some of them, it was one short one, but they were fairly good size, and my two sisters were larger than I. So I thought it was… I felt real bad about it. I was just awfully upset about that, and about being so small. Oh. And I remember one day my dad was sitting in a rocking chair, and I was… I don't know what I was doing. He picked me up on his lap. He said, You know, good things are done up in small packages. Well, I remember things like that more. I don't remember him ever getting after me. And I wasn't any angel either, because I was acting and climbing up on top of the house. I was told to get down.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But you didn't… It just seemed to me I don't think…

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: There wasn't any temptation. There wasn't any that I know of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your brothers and sisters that were bigger and older than you have any interest in bossing you around at all? Did they do that?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, this story I've told a good many times. A brother that's… He was five years older than I, and that's when I got older and was in town. We lived in town, and I… They learned to dance quite young. That was the recreation they had. And I learned to dance awfully young, because we had family dance. There's not family dances, but neighborhoods would come into your home when they dance. And they'd be maybe a banjo and a violin and piano or whatever. Oh, and I got into town. I, of course, wasn't a Missed Anne. I had one of this brothers, smarter than I was, and he knew a lot more than I did. And I don't care who I was going to date. He dated the girl, my girlfriend, and it was the four of us together. I was never out of his sight. And yet he never got after me for anything or ever did it in a way… Well, I just thought he was a wonderful brother. He was such a wonderful dancer that I was going to get after me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I was going to something, and he says, Who are you going with?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I told him, he says, No, you're not. I said, Well, I sure am. And so when this fellow wrapped at the front door, he went to the door and said, She's not going, and I didn't go. And I never said anything, because it wasn't doing any good. And I didn't want to say, because I never quarreled with any of them. But I didn't speak to him for a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At the end of that… Well, would you say anything like that today?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Indeed you don't.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But, of course, now everything… You don't know what you've been seeing on TV.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: So I… It was different.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He was really looking out to protect you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He was trying to protect you. Sure he was. He was going to be right where I was and see that I was taken care of. And he wasn't going to let me go with someone, and he thought I shouldn't. And he saw that I didn't, but the way he didn't, it worked out.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I think of him as an awful lot. And I think, No, I could have thanked him for taking care of me. But I didn't think he was taking care of me, but he was. You thought you were just going on double dates. Yes, that's what it was to me. I don't think a brother and a sister double date anymore. They do. I haven't seen it or heard of it in recent years. But he was every place that I went to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the kind of things that you would go to? Just be mainly dances and dating in those days?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They were public dances in town. There was a hall they had the public dances in, and everybody was there. And nearly everybody danced in. There wasn't any other recreation or not really. And they weren't out running around on the streets. Not that I know of. I never knew of it. Well, I heard that in some communities that some of the people were close to dancing, didn't want people to dance at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you heard about that?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: In some communities, some people didn't like dancing and didn't believe in it. Well, they did around in Genesee. I don't think there was anyone in Genesee that objected to dancing. Of course, the dancing was a lot different then than it is now. But I don't know what it is now. I don't know. But there was, as far as I know, if you go to a dance now, there's more or less drinking, and they all do. But I don't believe that they ever did then. And I remember as I got older that, you know, that then once in a while, there'd be someone up there that had a little too much to drink. Because I know one night this man had always been very nice to me, and he was a beautiful dancer. And he started over to, came over to ask me for dance. My brother knew he was coming to get a dance, and he walked right around him and in front of him real quick, and he says, Come on, this is our dance. He didn't think I should be dancing with him. So I didn't. But he just did it so quickly that, and there wasn't a word said. He never said a word to me. He didn't say, Well, he's been drinking. You can't dance with him. He never said anything about it. And I knew I was old enough then, and I knew that fellow hadn't been drinking.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But you would have danced with him if he'd asked you anyway, probably.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't know what I would have done. I really don't know whether I would have got up and danced with him or not. I had no idea, because there was so little of it. You just didn't see anything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in those dances, did you, did a person that usually danced with almost everybody else that was there during an evening? Is that how they went?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: You didn't dance with one person all evening, which I understand they do a great deal. Yes, they do. And then you didn't. You danced with your friends, all of them. They were all their friends.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, it's just so different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they asked you just right before the dance?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: We just— Well, when the music would start, well, then the men would start over where the women, they were sitting down and come up and ask for a dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And once in a while, before the music started, if you had to be standing around somewhere, or close to one that wanted a dance, may I have the next dance?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But that was all. He wasn't asked only just the next dance. I don't know when this other started, really, because it seemed like it kind of came on so gradually that—

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Oh, it was just different.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I never—as I said, I—and I was no angel. I was into things and done things that I shouldn't. I know that that. Like when I climbed up on the house, I knew I shouldn't. I had a pin up there, but I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get punished for doing it?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No. He just saw me and told me to get down on the house. I got down. And there was no fuss made about it and nothing more sad. That was it.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And now, why the friction between the parents all the time. You tell a child, he can't have it. They're going to do it anyway. And they look up out of the corner of their eye and keep going on starting to do it. I don't remember ever seeing anything like that when I was a child. Never.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I wonder why the kids were so obedient then.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't know whether it had anything to do with us being living on the ranch, because we did have— we went back and forth. You spoke of Tom Wall. Well, they were—of course, Tom, I knew his mother. His mother was a wonderful woman. And I came from a large family, and I loved to go over there because she was a real fussy person. And she was the only person I knew that was always dressed up. She never wore a house dress like other women around there. And she had a little white apron that she wore when she was getting the meal. And the meals were served while I was in my seventh heaven when I could go over there. And she'd ask me to stay a day or two, an idea that I could go over and stay. She gave me a piano lesson. She had some beautiful paintings and things. I told Tom, I said, I know more about your mother than you do, because he didn't know her until later. And she had a family. And they—I don't know whether you know or not, but the one child was crippled. And, of course, I think he was passed away about 20 years. So he was fairly young anyway. And she spent all of her time with him instead of on her house. She took care of him. He couldn't take care of himself. And so she—Tom doesn't know his mother being so immaculate, as I do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: SHORT - When you knew her, was that before she had this baby that was crippled?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: BERRY Yes. Yes, that's when they were—before they had any children, that I used to go there a lot. But after she had her family, and Calis, of course, was crippled. He was born that way. I guess it was an injury. I really don't know the particulars. But anyway, she had her hands full.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: SHORT - Could the child walk at all?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Or could he— BERRY Well, he got so he got around some.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: he couldn't even—well, she had to put him to bed. And if you wanted to turn over, she had to turn him over in bed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: SHORT - Did she ever feed him too?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: BERRY Well, I can't remember that she fed him or she didn't. But I know that she had to turn him over in bed and that she was—her time was spent with Calis. And I imagine I would say that he might have, as he got older, could feed himself. But I kind of question that. I just can't remember that. SHORT - So that would have been really having her hands full. BERRY Oh, it was terrible. It really was. And he was—sorry, he wasn't exactly deformed, but he was in a way, too, in Calis, had fun, you know. Well, you would think at a glance that he just didn't have a brain. And he really had a—he was very smart, but he couldn't pass it on.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He couldn't express himself. But those that really knew, of course, I was so young that I didn't, you know, it didn't impress me too much. Well, it did, because I felt sorry for them.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But I can't remember him ever doing anything on his own that he was helped. But in later years, they got a car for him, and he did drive. And I know people were kind of shocked. They didn't think he should be on the road. He might have had better control of these arms than he did of these legs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But on the other hand, how could he have too good a control of these arms, or why couldn't he have turned over and bent?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't know, because— JANET But he was smart. BERRY One, they said he was brilliant, really. But if you didn't really know him, you wouldn't know. Because it was hard for him to talk, he couldn't express himself. It didn't come out the way he wanted to.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: JANET Well, I wanted to ask you about Mrs. Wall being immaculate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wouldn't that be kind of rough to do in the country farms in those days?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: BERRY She's on the woman's lot. JANET Living was pretty rough then, anyway. BERRY No, not for her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: JANET How could she ask that?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: BERRY They had a big porch, and they had a long dining table in there. And at that time, the farmers had hired help, because the machinery and everything was so different. They had to have quite a bit of— And when she had hired men, she fed them out there, because her house, in the kitchen, in the living room, she didn't want their dirty clothes in there. I remember that very well. And I know that Tom took me down to show me his son's new home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, do you remember where your mother had this kitchen built, or this room built off in the kitchen to feed the hired help? He laughed, because he didn't— JANET Do you think that she could be so immaculate because she had help in the home?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No. BERRY She did that all herself, the whole housework. JANET She did it herself. About the only help that I ever remember, at that time, you couldn't go buy clothes like you do now. And she had a dressmaker come up from, I think it was Lois, but she was in jealousy anyway. It wasn't in jealousy. And she had this woman come up and sew, and she'd be there for a week or two, and she'd make these fancy aprons and things for her. And that's the only help I ever remember. In later years, I wasn't around her so much. And Kellis got worse, and she'd taken him to doctors in different places and so forth. I wasn't around her as much. But she got—so she wasn't like that. She was more like ordinary person. For instance, she came by one day, so many people wrote a horse back. Well, she had been someplace, and she come by. She got up, tied her horse up in front, came out with me, setting on the porch. And when she got off, the horse rubbed his head against her. She says, I should have been—she said, I should have gone home and changed my shirt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, well, why?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, there's no excuse for dirt. Even if that horse just did it, she should have gone on home and changed. Just there's no excuse for dirt.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But you found her place, her home, to be a very special place to you, too. It was very special to me. It certainly was. And she was—I just thought the world of her. And you didn't go to your neighbors like you do—well, you could now, because you didn't have the transportation. Because, as I said, if you either got to get a horse or walk quite a distance—

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And Tom's grandmother was hard of hearing, and she had one of those horns. I bet you've never seen them. No, I've seen pictures. And she held that up to her ear, and you talked in that. And I always wanted to take a hold of it. I was curious to get a hold of that. I wouldn't—it would show me a kid in the day that wouldn't. If they were curious, they'd go up and they'd ask her questions, and they'd want to touch it. But for some reason, I didn't have the nerve.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I always wanted to take a hold of that horn and take a look at it, because I just wondered, how did she get to hear it through that and what it was?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I didn't know what it was all about. I knew what she was using it for, but that's all. But I never touched it.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I like to tease my wife sometimes. She can't hear in one ear. Sometimes I tell her she should have one of those horns.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: There's an awful lot of that in here, hard hearing. Well, it's been that way with her ever since she was a child. Oh, for goodness sake. Well, that's really good to have. Bad enough when it comes on and you get older.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you know, what made that place her house so special to you?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: What was it that made her place so—I mean, it was clean, and it was the culture, too, I—from what you said.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: She was a marvelous person, and they were such kind people. A husband, too. Well, he was a—oh, I don't know. They just seemed like such wonderful people to me. And they were so good to me. And I was always welcome.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And—well, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far away was it from your home?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Oh, I—I suppose a mile and a half or close to two miles. She just walked. Over through the field. Over there on the road, you go a little farther.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But they were just wonderful people, and I think all the rest of the neighbors were. I never heard—and even after I was older, and you began—and we moved into town, and I heard things about people in town. I never heard of any of those people out there ever doing things that they shouldn't. Or they never—well, they just didn't, I don't think. I don't think they did. Maybe she had so much time. Maybe she had time to spend with you because she didn't have any kids at that time. I wonder if that was— Well, she'd spend a lot of time on her home.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But I think one thing she knew that I thought the world of her, and I loved music, and she— I started going over there. I just don't know. I haven't any idea.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Because, well,

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: time meant nothing. I never thought of Angel Guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how close was your family to the other families around, to the other neighbors?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, as far as I know, they were all good friends.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And, of course, we had cousins that lived on out farther east. It was about three or four miles, and it was a large family too. And we'd go over there. But not real often because we both— They were two big families, and they couldn't all go.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: So maybe just a few would go at a time, but you just didn't go very often. And yet, I never heard them ever say an unkind word. I never heard that one of the girls or boys ever say anything about their sister or brother or give them heck for anything they've done. Never, ever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when they socialized your family, how did they do it? Did they visit another family, or did they get together at the school, or what did they do?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, they didn't really. I guess considering socializing, they wasn't very much of that. We went to things, like I said, when they had a program in Pine Grove, we'd go down there. But really, there wasn't much talent, because no one— But there wasn't any way. You couldn't— There was no teachers to teach any talent, anything unusual. At school, the only thing I can remember if I was any funds was when they had a spelling beat every Friday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would that mean to a kid today?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They'd be bored.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, you didn't seem to— Somehow, you got along, and you were happy with what you had. And if you had a horse, and quite often, each one would have their own horse. Or maybe, like the three of us that were younger, we all got on one horse. Or maybe there'd be just one on the song at a time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would we ride that horse around?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They did have these country dances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At a person's home?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: In their home, because I know we had them in our home.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: It wasn't in piano then, I don't think. I'd never heard of a piano. We had an organ. And my oldest brother played the violin, or fiddle they called it them, because they never had a lesson. He just picked it up. And my sister played the organ. And then the other fella played—I think it was a banjo, but I'm not even sure of that. One of the neighbors. And the whole family would come. There wouldn't be too many. The houses were built. The kitchens were always large, and the living room seemed to me that most of them were pretty good size. And they danced, and I thought they had a good time.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And how they get together could—I don't know. I don't understand. I know Tom and I talked about it different times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would it stay? Would it be a long evening? Some dance into the early hours of the morning?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't think so. I really don't.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: It would possibly be between 12 and 1 o 'clock, maybe 1 o 'clock. But I doubt if there would be anything after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would there be eats?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't remember being. Because I don't—like at our house, mother had a large family, but she couldn't serve to all these people. I don't think that any of them did. I can't remember that.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't think they—I just don't think they did.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't remember ever eating at a dance until after we moved into Genesee, and then they'd go—there was a restaurant there, and the kids would go up there and eat so far. But in the country, I don't think they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did all ages come to go to the Genesee dances, just like the country dances? Or was it more the same age in town?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, I think they were all there. Kids not quite as young as they did in the country, because in the country they didn't leave them at home. I don't think they did. No, it was different. It was altogether different. Because I know that—I don't remember seeing any small youngsters at a dance. It was in town.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't believe that women took small youngsters and went to things like that. I think they—when we first moved to town, they kind of more stayed home with their kids. You didn't leave them with babysitters.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And you didn't bring them with you either, like you did in the country. No, I didn't.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, I don't remember. It doesn't run in my mind of ever seeing a youngster there. In later years, I did jazz. But when I was young, and I think they averaged almost a dance a week, because that was about the only amusement they had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In town?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And then maybe be in the country. Somebody built a new barn. They'd have a barn dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there other things like clubs that you took part in in town and Genesee besides the dances when you were young?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, eventually. You did, of course.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I didn't know—or I knew there were clubs. But actually, I was never in playing cards or anything like that until after I was married. And of course, I went along with the group and went to card clubs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what the clubs were mostly? Card clubs?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Only with card clubs. There were quite a few in Genesee that played cards and pretty good card players there, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they women's clubs or for—?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, sometimes there'd be a group that would farm where they would baby, be three or four couples, four or five or so, you know, have so many tables. But it's more women's clubs. Now, when I was younger, I was with a group, and we had four or five tables. We played cards, and the men were there.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But in recent year, I don't believe they did that anymore. There's maybe a couple get together and go and play cards with each other, but not as a group.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those clubs affiliated with, you know, like national women's clubs, or were they just local?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Just local.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your folks have a 160 -acre farm out there, or was there—?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Oh, that arm, I don't know.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: My dad homesteaded. They called it the Reservation. And my brother owns that farm now. And now, I don't know. I've always been going to ask my brother, I wanted to see the deed to it, because it's supposed to be a little different. It was signed by the president at that time. And I don't think the president signs the deeds anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I've always think, well, why didn't I ask Bob to see that?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: In fact, I never even thought of it until in later years, and one time it was mentioned. Oh, I never had seen it. But he homesteaded this, and the Indians rode a horseback. They came up over the hills, and they were moored around through Lapawee. And they had a trail came up there, and it came right close to my dad's place, where they were living then.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And, well, he, I don't know how long after he homesteaded that, he bought some more land.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: That was a couple or three miles from there, and where they built a home. But the Indians used to come through, and for some reason, they took a lightning to my dad. And they called him Chief Roberts. They didn't, no one ever called him Bob when he was young. They called him Robert. And they called him Chief Roberts. But he kept a horse saddled, they said. And I don't remember that, because, but I was told later that they did keep a horse saddled. But that wasn't done in any good, because of the Indians, there was more than one, there was nothing they could do anyway. They couldn't even horse out for it to get away if he needed to. I guess so. But they never bothered us at all. Or they'd come and maybe ask for something, you gave it to them. But I really didn't see much of them.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They wouldn't abuse that, they would just ask once in a while for something.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't think it happened very often. But, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You gave it to them to keep on their good side?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes, you'd have been about to ask, you just did it if you had it. You didn't why you told them you didn't have it. But I don't think that people ever misrepresented to other people. I think that they just told them the truth.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't think it ever occurred to try to lie out of anything. Or maybe I wasn't around, and that's what I heard, and I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that your father would treat the Indians just the same as he would treat the white people, or would he treat them different?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He was friendly with them as much as he could be, but they didn't mix much.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I know that they didn't have any trouble, my dad didn't have any trouble, whatever, at any time with the Indians. And in later years, when I was a little older and could go a little farther, there was a family lived about a mile from there, and his wife was a full -blooded Indian. And I don't know, I'd heard different stories, how she learned to cook, she didn't know how to cook, Indians didn't know.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And anyway, they had two girls and a boy. And we were good friends, and I used to go down there. I'd walk down, and this Mrs. Williams would be sitting out on the front porch with her feet straight out, just like the Indians said, there she was.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And how she could sit there, and why?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: When they had chairs, but there's where she was.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Hello, Marowy, how are you?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Becky, he in the house. That was about all she'd say. She was friendly, she was not the least bit unfriendly, and I'm sure she was glad that we came whenever we did or anything. And the daughter was an immaculate housekeeper, and very well respected. And well, all three of them were the two girls and the man, the boy, and the family, all were very well respected. And when she had a family, and her girl got old enough in her high school, of course she'd gone to the country school, her mother was a little leery of sending her into genocide to school because there wasn't any Indians in there. Even though this daughter was only a half grade. So they had decided, I don't remember not just where it was, but they were going to send her to some school that was Indian. They thought maybe that she might be embarrassed, or they'd say something to her that they shouldn't. Well, it was a good friend of theirs that liked them, and glad to have her. And so this woman told Mrs. Williams that she'd heard her name of Cameron then. Mrs. Cameron, she said, I'd be glad to have her to stay with us and go to school. And she went through high school, and she was treated just like any other white person. And her sister did too. They both graduated from high school. And the oldest one, you wouldn't know she had any Indian love, but the younger girl looked more like an Indian.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that this woman, it seems she had this friend in Genesee accepted her completely, didn't mind at all that she was Indian to take her daughter?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, because the family were respected by all good people. Everybody thought they were just real nice people. And this woman that took her in was a piano teacher, and she was a good worker in the church. And Neoma came in there and stayed with her and attended church and everything else right along with one of the family, and she was accepted. And she came back, and years later I'd seen her a few times. And I don't think anyone ever thought of her as being part Indian. And I never her mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there were some people that wouldn't have liked her mother because of that? Because she was a full -blooded Indian, married to a white?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I don't know. I don't believe any of the neighbors out there ever said or done anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she come and take part in dances and like other people?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, her mother didn't.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Her mother is the one I meet.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, she didn't.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But, well, of course, Rebecca, she would be a half -breath because her father was a white man. Well, she married a white man. And their family was accepted as white people and associated with them. And I don't think, of course, they don't live there anymore. And wherever they went, I can't help but think they still passed off as white people. I don't think anybody would ever think of anything different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, weren't there a fair number or at least some?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, there were some people that were just like this woman that were Indians and married whites.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, that was more around through maybe Black Boy. I think it happened down there. But up in our country, there was only this one family that I knew of that married an Indian.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But they didn't mix really then. That family didn't mix with the other families.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: On a social basis. Well, there wasn't such a thing as a social basis of those kind, really. But Mrs. Williams didn't go uncalled on her neighbors. All I can remember of her is it sitting out on that front porch. And she never learned to really do housework and to cook like she should have. Maybe, I don't know how they did before they had this daughter that was old enough to do the work. Because I wasn't in their house and I was younger, so much younger. And as I got older, they were accepted. And I never even thought to ask my mother.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Because my mother probably could have told me more about it, but it never occurred to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they seem to have a happy marriage?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I think so. I really do. Because they all seem to be so kind of easygoing. And I was there quite a bit. And I never ever heard any of them ever say an unkind word to any of them. Each other. The sisters of the brother, the mother of the father. And of course, I didn't talk to the mother very much. She was a little hard to talk to.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And he was just like any other white man. I didn't think anything about it. I thought it was perfectly all right. Because people didn't talk about those things. In this day and age, everybody would be talking about it. And it gives you think about it and you'd probably ask them questions. Oh no, but they were a second and that was it. And when Rebecca, of course, as I said, she was a hat -free, started at a country school. They were all white people. And she was the only one that wasn't. Well, as far as I was concerned, she was white. And I knew she was and I knew her mother was an Indian. But there wasn't any difference between her and any of the others. She was just a real nice person.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But I don't remember her ever being to any of those country dances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Rebecca?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Now whether she was or not, I don't know. But I don't remember of ever seeing her at a dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think maybe the family would feel hesitant about it?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: She herself may have. Because she was a little hesitant about sending her to school in jealousy. Because they were all white people. So my idea would be that Rebecca herself might have felt. I don't know. Because it was never, ever anything sad. But now Rebecca is the one that went to school in jealousy. No, she was a hat -free.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it was her daughter?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: That's her daughter. I see. That went to school in jealousy. But it was Rebecca's mother that sat on the porch. Yes. I don't know what her name was. I haven't any idea. To me, she was Mrs. Williams.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: When I was a kid, you didn't call anyone married woman by the first name. It was Mrs. I didn't know their names.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, Rebecca.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She married and lived and just stayed right there?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, she married a Tennessee man. And they bought a farm where good farmers made a good living.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Retired?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Or they're in a retirement place. Oh, I don't know just where it is. I've never been over there between here and Seattle. And it's quite a nice home. I've heard others speak about it. And they thought it was a real nice place. It had a very good reputation. And I don't know. As I understand it, you had your own apartment. And you could if you wanted to fix your meal there. Or you could go and eat in the dining room. You had a choice.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: So Rebecca went to school in the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she go on to high school?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In jealousy?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yeah. Her daughter. But it wasn't her that they were going to send away to school. It was her daughter. It was her daughter.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: That she was afraid that she wouldn't be a septum.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But she married a white man. Rebecca did. They all did.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes, she did. She married a white man. And I know at the time, it seemed kind of strange to me. I thought, gee, he's marrying her. She is a white woman. He's a white man. But I really hadn't been around the Indians at all. Because they were mostly in Lackaway. And I don't remember when they used to come up through there, ride up through there, and do this war -hooping like they do. And so on. And they did a lot of it out there. But I don't remember much about it. I think I was too young to remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you grow up being afraid at all of Indians? Did you grow up being afraid of Indians at all?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Not at all. No, no. One bit. To me, they were just like anyone else. I think that if... And when I was young, if an Indian had asked me for a date, I'd been embarrassed. I think that would have bothered me. Because I wouldn't want to be disrespectful to him. And I would hate to admit that I wouldn't go out with him. But I don't believe I would. Because I really was never around an Indian. Because to me, Rebecca was a half -breed, but she was a white woman. Far as I was concerned, she's just like any other white person.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Did you and she...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you two friends?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes, we were good friends. And I never even thought of her as being an Indian. Because she was treated just like any other white person. And everybody, she... Well, everyone respected her.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: She went to school in Genesee herself.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, you know, when you say that she wondered about her daughter going to school there, it makes me think that maybe she didn't have the easiest time herself. Well, it could be the things that I wouldn't have known about. Maybe a few things came up. And she kept it to herself. I wouldn't know. I just don't know.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: She probably didn't...

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: When...

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: As you knew her, probably she didn't have a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Her whole life was with white people, not with Indians, probably, right?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: She was never around any entirely at all. I don't think. They might have come up from Laffley and come up... Some up there were... Now, then, but I... I rather doubt it.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And she must have dated this white... The white man that she married. I mean, they must have gone together some. Well, I suppose they did. I really don't know.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Because it was never discussed or I ever heard anything about it. And whether he just... Wade laws were and so forth. Whether he just married her and saw her and married her and that was it. I don't imagine that he really kept company with her any length of time. I shouldn't say that I don't think so because I don't know. I have no idea. Because I never saw much of the Indians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that people had the idea then that Indians were kind of inferior at the time?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Because the way they lived and the way they acted.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well... I don't know. I never really had any prejudice against them in any way. And in later years, I knew a couple of girls from Blackway that were full blooded Indians and were lovely girls. Only they had a drinking habit. And that, of course, that's one thing that Indians do like their liquor. They had a little trouble there. One of the girls that I knew was...

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I think the Chief Joseph was her grandfather. And her father's name was Joseph, too.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He tried to talk to the Indians when they get a sum of money just by land. You know, and trying to get ahead. And she told me herself that you couldn't do anything with them. Said it didn't mean anything to them. Said they got this money and they just drank it and that was it. And they just lived in anything that... Well, I haven't been in Blackway for several years, but they didn't used to be hardly. Well, they were just old shacks, you might say, mostly. And I think they just didn't care. But I think in the now that they've spread out more and they're around through the country, they're probably a lot different. But I know Josephine used to tell me quite a bit about them. That, well, she just didn't think they had any ambition and they just thought the government should keep them. But she had ambition herself. And she wasn't that type and neither was her father. And her father was a full -blooded Indian.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: So... But she didn't go along with it at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was Joseph's daughter, Josephine?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was her mother a white?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I never... No, her mother was an Indian. I saw her once and she was a full -blooded Indian. And, of course, Josephine dressed like anyone else. But one day I saw her mother and she introduced me to her. And I remember she had little scarves tied around like the Indians do. And I was very much surprised because I expected her mother to be more like a white person. Because Josephine was a nice looking girl and very neatly dressed and everything. I know this surprised me when I saw this scarf tie around her mother's head. But I had always thought that I'd go to a life away while Josephine was there and see where they lived. Because she had asked me out, but I never did. I guess maybe I was a little reluctant because they were a full -blooded Indian family.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I don't think I'd have the courage to walk in. If there were more Indians there, I wouldn't feel at home. He had a half -brother. His mother had been married before and evidently lost her husband in Scotland. And then she married Gray.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And then she had two sons and a daughter. There were Grays. And they came... Well, I can't tell you. I'd have to look it up. I haven't written down when they... But they came out to Salt Lake City. I don't know. You may be a Mormon for all I know. Oh, I'm not. Well, at that time, the Mormons didn't like the other people. And my grandfather went to Salt Lake City. And I don't... It must have been just kind of a little shack that he had. And he worked with harnesses and things like that. Kind of a harness shop. And the Mormons run them out of Salt Lake City. They didn't want anyone else in there. So he went to Carson City from Salt Lake City. And then my dad and my uncle came up to Walla Walla. And they stayed there for a while. And they came on over to Genesee. And they both homesteaded farms out there.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And their farms were a few miles apart. But they both homesteaded.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And they got that land just when the Indian Reservation opened up for settlement.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know whether you paid anything for land then, didn't you?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Just have to get...

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I had to leave that for five years, I think, and do so many improvements. And then it was yours. There was a filing fee. But I don't know if there was anything else.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I've never heard that mention. And I never even thought to ask. If they did, they didn't pay much because he didn't have any money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think he was there waiting?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I know a lot of people were waiting at Genesee for the land to open up. And then they just took what they could.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I don't know. Really, they were homesteading it when he and my uncle came. And when it happened, when they opened up, I don't know. But they actually homesteaded the land. But they actually homesteaded it. 200 acres is a little over your little under item. It's a little over because some of it has a little canyon land in it. They didn't farm land. And he homesteaded that. And as I said, the thing that always... I've been curious about is the deed to this. I think that that should be framed.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I suppose my uncle's was the same thing was probably signed by the president because it was bought the same time. Then of course they bought more land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did he meet your mother? Did he know her before he came there? Did he meet your mother before he came there or after?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, my mother came from Nebraska. And her father, the mother, came out and they homesteaded the place down towards the Pine Grove district down there.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But I really don't know what her folks, what nationality are even.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But I just don't know where the fallouts came from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But related to maleen, fallot?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Distant, we don't claim any relation because it... But in one time, the parents, I guess, kind of got together and found out there was a distant relation in the fallot. And just where maleen's mother and father came from, I've never talked to maleen about it because we've never really said much ever discussed it. I never thought of them as being related. Because I didn't know it for so long. When it came up in later years and making a difference to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it rough going?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: You say you had no money when you started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it difficult in the early years for him on the farm?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't think it's as difficult as it is now because people now have to ask so much and demand so much. And their children demand so much. Nobody needed it. Nobody had it.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And of course, I think it is... Well, you lived on a farm. You raised everything you ate. You had pigs. You had cattle. You had chickens. So you had your bread and you baked your bread. You didn't buy it, that's for sure. And you raised your garden. And I remember it was an orchard back of the house. And my dad had a big pit. They dug this pit. One was for potatoes and vegetables that you could keep were put in different pits. Well, these pits were lying with straw for the vegetables so they wouldn't be in the dirt. Then you put straw over and then dirt over that. You'd go out there and dig out enough of whatever you wanted. Well, I don't think you ever bought or used to speak of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By how deep were those pits? I don't mean exact, but I mean, would they be deep enough you could stand in it?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, no, they weren't too deep because he had, of course, more than one. Oh, yeah. There would be one for potatoes and carrots. Oh, I see there's many vegetables you couldn't put in a pit. And I don't know whether they can vegetables in or not. That don't register with me. I can't remember because I don't remember seeing any fruit jars.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I think in those earlier days, they weren't. I don't think so.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, to me, there wasn't. And I remember they had a back of the house with it. He dug quite an end to a bank. And then the line that was something I don't know what, so that the dirt wouldn't come down into it. And in the summertime, you had to put your milk out there in pans.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Because you couldn't keep it in the house. You had to have milk because everybody ate bread and milk or drank milk.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But you've had, we, I don't know, there was never any lack of anything because we had plenty of meat because we had our own beef. And we had the pigs and everything. And they did their own butchering. I can remember seeing the meat hung up. It was frozen. I think the winters, to me, the winters were more severe. They were colder. You could keep meat for quite a long time. But maybe that's just the, I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did other people say that, that the winters were colder?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I would say so. And so we, oh, we sat. And of course we had all of this proof. But there was no way, I think maybe, I would say, but I'm not sure of that either, that he probably had a pit of apples too. Because there were quite a few apples of different kinds. So I think there would be, because I know there was quite a few pits back there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, could he make his entire living by farming? Did he do anything else for a living besides the farm? Do you know if he'd farm before he came here?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Doesn't sound like it if he— No, I'm sure they didn't. I think he was pretty young. And as I said, he and my uncle came to Walla Walla and then came on up here. And I think they were fairly young and just went out there and homesteaded. And whether they got a little work then, but I don't think there was any work. I don't know where they'd work. No one else had any money around there. They could pay him for anything.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, you know, the reputation of the country around there is so good. I wonder whether you just couldn't get a pretty good crop off that land right from the start.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Oh, I think they did. I really do. I think they did. I think they did right at the beginning that— I don't know how they got a bot machinery. That's never even— I never even entered my mind to start out with because they'd have to have a plow where they didn't have machinery like they do now. But they had to have a mower and they had to have a plow. And a few things. And they had—I know Dan had quite a few horses. But before that, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he must have been able to do well enough from his farming to get the horses and the equipment that— When you were young, he had pretty good equipment, did he? Did he have what he needed to farm right?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He had all he needed. And of course, for that time, what I remember, he bought V Orland and built this home. Because when they first moved on this reservation, it was just kind of a little shack, two -room shack out there. And just when, or how long it was, that they bought this land and built a home, I don't know. Because the home was built when I was a baby. So that's the only home I was ever in, so we moved into Genesee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they live in the two -room shack until they built that home?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Just kind of a shack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that right here? Was that right near the other place?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No. They're about two or three miles apart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that on the reservation too?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No. It isn't. No, it isn't on the reservation. Well, it's near out there where Tom Wall's farm is. And I really just don't know how they managed. Of course, the machinery didn't cost very much then. But I know they paid for it somehow. When there was no, you couldn't go out and get a job, I'm sure of that. So I don't know how they managed.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I just don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your mother? Did she have to do a lot of work on the place, a lot of responsibility?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Just her housework. She never went out in the field or anything like that. Or I never saw her milk a cow or she had enough to do to take care of the family and do the cooking. That was a full -time job. So it was a fairly good -sized house.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Because there was two bedrooms and a big kitchen and a pantry and a living room downstairs besides the bedrooms upstairs. So it wasn't a small house. And he built that about the time I was born.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But I really just don't know what they did before. But when I was old enough to know what was going on, we had these animals. By that time, I probably started coming out with jars not too long after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember if there was cash in your family? You had money that you could buy much with when you were growing up?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Nobody wanted anything. You didn't demand things like you do today. No, we, well, for instance, a bedroom very, I don't think in the house was that old. Ever had a clothes closet. There was a bar about so wide, nailed on the wall back of the door, and you hung up your clothes on that. I never saw a coat hanger or anything like that when I was a child. And after we moved to town, we had clothes closets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that because you just didn't have hardly any clothes to put the hanger up? Was that it?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: People didn't have a lot of any clothes. I suppose I probably had, I might have had two or three dresses and you'd wash them. You didn't have a lot. That's for sure. Because they only had this behind the door where they hung their clothes. And I know I was talking to someone not too long ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, do you remember when we used to hang our clothes behind the door?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: We didn't even have clothes closets. I sure do.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: What would you do without a clothes closet?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Clothes closets aren't big enough now for all the clothes people have got.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, they didn't demand it. Nobody seemed to care because no one else had it. They weren't trying to outdo the other one. And I think that's one reason why they got along so well. No one was jealous of anyone or had, there was no, I don't think there was any ill feeling.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But not having clothes, did that mean that, you know, I've heard them say how the town kids used to make fun of the country kids.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They'd say, well, the kids, they don't change their clothes, they smell you, they're not clean like you. You know, I think maybe that was in later years.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Because I don't think that happened. Where, well, I know it wasn't in our family because I know they took their baths. And I think that anyone in our family came in from the field and needed a bath. They got it. They took it.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But again, on the other hand, they could have been some older, and maybe I was kind of accustomed to it and expected and never thought of it. But I can't hardly believe that I would be accustomed to it. I just can't. Because I know that they all did the same thing, as I said. They'd have these wash tubs, they'd call them, and they had their baths.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: You probably had to do wash every week if you only had so little clothes, just a few dresses. They're probably pretty often that they wash.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I think we, I'm sure that they washed more than once a week. All born. Well, I'm sure they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But do you think that your folks were taking the money that he made farming and putting it into more land? Do you think that's what they were doing?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Oh, they bought more land, yes.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He bought... I don't know. I think they supposed, was it 120 in one field and 80 in another, something like this, where they built the home later, where they live, not where the home says.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And now they're going to tear down that house. The man has bought the course of this land that's been sold, and he's built a new home out there. And I said to Tom Wallen by there, I said, you know, I've always wanted to go back there and go inside of that house again. He said, I'll take you over sometime. I said, oh, no, I was... Because they built a new home now out, a great big one out in front of it. Well, did that mean that if your mother had only the housework to do, did she have leisure, and she had time to... There was no leisure time. I think she was busy all the time. With housework and... I don't remember of ever seeing her in the garden. She might have gone down and got some once in a while. But I can remember going down. She'd tell me, go down and get some carrots or this or that. And I can see Dad standing down there, holding the garden. That he spent a lot of time holding the garden. And because the older boys were doing quite a bit of the farm work. And I don't remember mother doing anything like that at all. But leisure, though. She didn't have time to sit down and just relax and... I don't remember of ever seeing her sit down and relaxing. And I just don't remember of her seeing her. Doesn't surprise me because I've heard other people say the same. I don't remember ever seeing her sit down relaxing. And of course, she didn't have magazines. I don't think we had a daily paper. Not sure they even had a mailman come out, I remember there must have been. But I just don't recall anything about any mailman or anything. They did later years. But as a child, I don't remember. Because I just don't think there was a great deal of mail. I think we'd go to town not very often. But they did go in now and then. And they probably picked up what mail was if there was any. But I know we didn't have magazines or papers. When I was real small and on the ranch. Of course, later years we did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot that you were expected to do? Was there a lot of chores that you were expected to do? Or did your older brothers and sisters take care of that stuff?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No. I helped with auditions. I always dusted. That was one of my jobs. And I milked a column, but not because I was told who. But I see I had several brothers older. And my sister was quite a bit older. So we weren't very close. I mean, we weren't together a lot because she was so much older than I. And the boys did the milking. And so either it was a college. I wanted to milk. I thought that cow should be my cow. And I could milk her. And they let me. I'd take my family and go down and milk her. Maybe they milked part of it. Maybe if I didn't do a good job, they'd finish it. I don't know if they did. I didn't pay attention. But I milked a cow quite a while.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Because we always had cats. And you'd squirt the milk and the cat.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Feed the cat while he was milking. But I did quit milking. I didn't keep that up too long. I'm sure of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you have as much time as you wanted to play? Do what you wanted to that way?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Sure.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: You weren't very - We rode horses more than anything else. We'd get on a horse and ride to the neighbors. Maybe ride them up and down the bank somewhere.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I know there was a bank. They told me not to go up that bank. I shouldn't. I thought, well, I bet I do. And they did.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But if they knew it, they did. They never followed me out. Sounds like they teach you pretty good then, really. But there was one thing I ever pulled over on them, and then I never told them. Dad had, you know, there would be two horses that would go together. They were in pairs. Well, there was a pair of gray horses. And one was a real gentle horse, and the other wasn't. There ain't no one we could get on his back. He'd bust them off right now.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And it didn't make sense to me. And of course, the barns were built with the stalls and the wall up between. And I wondered why I couldn't climb up that wall and slip over on this horse that he wouldn't even know I was there. And I did. He never moved. I sat there. If I'd kicked him, you know, we're done in the same. He probably would have thrown me off.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But I sat there quite a while, and I thought, what's wrong with this horse?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He's just gentle as the other one. And then pretty soon I slid off, got down, went out of the barn. I never told him what I did, because I knew. I was afraid then I would get into trouble. Someone didn't tell him.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: You know, it sounds like you were really treated well by your parents.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I mean, you weren't afraid of really afraid of them hurting you or hitting you or seems like you felt free to be somewhat independent, which sounds good to me. Well, I think they all were. And the people that I knew were all the same way. They were different families where I used to get on a horse, you know, and go over. And as far as I know, it was all the same. I don't think there was any disputes to speak. There might have been once in a while, but I just can't hardly believe it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think husbands and wives got along in those days?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I never heard one of them ever say anything back to the other one. No, well, they just say it. That's all. But I never heard it. And I know that I was in different families like a going over to my uncle's place. Well, I never heard them ever say anything to each other. I mean, to contradict one another or anything. I never heard them. And there was different families where I used to go. And I would be there maybe all day. No, I didn't pay too much attention to the parents because I was there to play with the kids. But I never heard anything. Never. Never was the word of disagreement.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They must have at times had disagreements. I don't know. But.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think here? Do you think, well, in other words, you figure that they kept it to themselves or they just would talk to each other in private?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes, they did. They kept it to themselves.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They sure did. And then they don't anymore. No, that's true. No, and I never, as I said, even with all the brothers that I had and everything, I never was really, they really never got after me or was strict with me in any way. And I said that this brother was taking care of me in a roundabout way and that I didn't even know what he was doing. He was just being nice to me as far as I was concerned.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I have understood that in some families that say the women were treated as well as in others, like some families a wife would wind up having to cut her own wood or even have to work in the field and that sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that looked down upon among you?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: There wasn't anyone around there that did that that I know of. Of course, I had the brothers to do things like that with my dad. I didn't think women split ones. And the few neighbors that I went to, I don't think any of the women that I knew, they may have, but I don't believe they ever did. But I may not have known what was going on. I was going to ask you a little bit too about the, well, some of the merchants in Genesee.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Now, I understand that Rosenstein's were a big store there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you deal with them at all, to trade with them?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yeah, I remember very well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of that family?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, they were just...

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: The only difference that I could see, they were, someone had told me they were Jews and I didn't know what a Jew was. And I don't know if that's true or not. Oh, they were. That's what I understand. Well, that's what I was told. And I wondered why it would make any difference. I didn't see what the difference was whether they were Jews or not. And I've been in their home and we traded at their store. But of course, they left there. I was still pretty young when they left. I think they went to California.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But I don't know if they mix very much with the other people. Oh, whether they didn't want to, or whether they were afraid they wouldn't be accepted or not. I don't know. I haven't any idea because to me, they were just another family. You don't know whether they did or didn't mix. You went in Genesee when you went there. Well, they were a little older than I. And I wasn't with their girls. I can't remember how many there were in the family or anything now. Or about the age of my older sister. And my older sister was a friend of one of them. But they didn't go back and forth a lot. But I know they were good friends. But I really just don't. I thought there was something different about it. And I didn't know why or what it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About them as a family?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I just thought that it was strange. It seemed to be a feeling. And I don't know whether it was that they were Jews and were a little reluctant to mix with the other people. Or whether the other people, I think the other people were accepted. If they were raised like I was, I wouldn't have made a difference to me whether they were Jews or not. And but I think maybe they kind of kept to themselves a little more. That's a feeling that I had. And I never saw Mrs. Rosenstein out very much, very few times. She seemed to kind of stay at home by herself, I thought a great deal. And of course the girls went to school and so forth.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But they were supposed to have a nice house for that time, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it that the family moved into Genesee? What was their thinking?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But the kids in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ran a place?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Oh, no. Dad bought a place. He bought a home in town. And we just moved in. And my brother farmed a ranch.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: So we left in town and then, the rest of our lives, lived in the same house and so on. Well, as we got married and left and so forth, that was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did your father then retire at that time?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He retired when he might have gone out a little in the summertime. But I don't remember him going out or thinking much about it again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was he older than that?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He was 10 years older than my mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he probably still, when you moved in town, what would he have been, about 50 or something?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: You know, I think my brothers did most of the work by the time we moved to town. He might have done a few things around, but not very much. He kind of retired. I really did retire.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And when we first moved into town, there was hardly any running water. Nearly everybody had a pump.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: There was a pump almost on, well, ours was on the back porch. That's where most of them were. But it wasn't very long that I remember we had the coils put in a stove and the tank put back in the stove, so we had a hot water tank.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Had bathtub and so forth. But I don't know what year that was or the thing about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of living in the town as compared to living in the country?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Oh, times began to change by the time we came to town.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: As I said, when we lived in the country, I never heard anyone saying anything about anybody. That was kind of a pleasure. And then after you got into town, you began to hear things.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: People talked more. And I guess they got to talk more all the time.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And that was really the only difference, as far as I know, because you had your own pleasure. You didn't have any money to spend, or you couldn't buy a lot of toys and things. You didn't have them.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: You might get a doll for Christmas.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Was there much difference in—sounds like you had more conveniences in town at least. Yes, we did, because shortly after we got to town, I think he started working on that probably right after we moved in and put the water in the house. So we had running water and can have a bath without taking it in the washtub.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that meant very much to like your mother, to have the conveniences?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes, sure it would mean quite a lot. The work was less. We did, he, she didn't—well, we didn't have a cow in town too for quite a while. They had, my dad had a barn, and quite a few people had a cow, a milk to cow for years. And sometimes we had two cows in there, but we had our own milk, and he still raised the animals on the ranch, did the butchering so far.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: But things have changed, considerable.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I don't know what a young person would do today if they were put back in that situation. They'd be lost, and we were perfectly happy.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: He didn't know any better.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, you would have lost the connection with knowing how to do things, knowing how to cope with it, because when you grew up, that's the way it all was. Yeah, that's right. Now we're so far away.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Yes, it is. No, you had to kind of do what had to be done.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And you couldn't hire things done. We didn't have a hired girl a time or two, but

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: not for very long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did—what year were you born in? What year were you born in?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: That you're asking my age.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Well, I thought I was being a little more delicate than that. Just think of it as for the historical record, really.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I was born in 97.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I was just trying to think of how old you would have been when you were getting through high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work at all at that time when you were in high school or finishing?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, very few people did. My sister, after she went to college and was teaching, she was gone a lot. When she came home in the summertime, she used to clerk in one of the stores, usually through the summer. But I never did. I helped more in the house with the housework.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After you finished high school, what did you do?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Got married right away.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: It's foolish to get married young like that, but sometimes they do.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They still do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think it's better to have some experience with living first before you get married, to grow up a little more?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, I don't.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: No, I think that people's times have just changed us all. It's different.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I've thought so many times that if my mother and dad would see what was going on today, I wonder what they'd think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think? I wonder what do you think they'd think?

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: They wouldn't believe it. I don't believe they could believe it.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And it took so little to satisfy a person.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: You didn't demand anything of people. I don't think they did. As far as I know, you got along with what you had. You knew that was it.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: I think I should get going. I've stayed quite a while. I think I should get going. I've stayed quite a while. I really enjoyed talking with you. In fact, I'd like to. If it'd be okay, maybe.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: Lola and Elizabeth. I said, I don't remember those things because it didn't mean anything to me. I thought, you know, everybody was the same.

speaker: Mary Grey Edwards
words: And I thought everybody told the truth. And I thought everybody was honest. I didn't think that anybody would take anything and didn't belong to them. We weren't raised that way. And I don't think any of them were then. I just don't know.
